original_hypothesis.title: Cross-Chain TVL Staleness Checks Defeated by Default Timestamps
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max if positions are initialized with the default timestamp, defeating any staleness checks for cross-chain TVL data.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: New holding positions are initialized with `positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max`. `getLatestUpdateTime` finds the minimum timestamp across all positions. If all positions have the default value, it returns `max`, which will pass any `timestamp > block.timestamp - threshold` freshness checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Minimum search over array containing type(uint256).max

preconditions: Vault relies on cross-chain TVL updates

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Returns max instead of block.timestamp when no valid timestamps exist
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check to ignore type(uint256).max during minimum search

analysis_questions: Does the accounting manager rely on getLatestUpdateTime for staleness checks?
Can stale cross-chain TVL data be used to manipulate share prices?
validation_requirements: Add a position with default timestamp

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: LayerZero messaging fails, causing cross-chain TVL data to become stale. The accounting manager checks freshness using getLatestUpdateTime. Because same-chain positions have max timestamps, the check passes. Attackers exploit the stale TVL to arbitrage share prices.
prerequisites: Delayed or failed cross-chain TVL update

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Arbitrage opportunities leading to value extraction

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for TVL staleness checks with default timestamps

validation_result.mechanism: sentinel value bypass in staleness check
validation_result.impact_summary: TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max when all positions have default timestamps, defeating any downstream staleness validation for cross-chain TVL data.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The defect stems from two interacting code patterns: (1) All new holding positions are initialized with positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max in Registry.sol's internal updateHoldingPosition function, and (2) TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime computes the minimum positionTimestamp across all positions but does not filter out the sentinel value type(uint256).max. The function has a fallback for latestUpdateTime == 0 (returning block.timestamp), but this fallback never triggers because the first position (the dummy at index 0, also initialized with type(uint256).max) immediately sets latestUpdateTime to type(uint256).max. Any staleness check of the form `getLatestUpdateTime() > block.timestamp - threshold` will always pass because type(uint256).max is astronomically larger than any block.timestamp-based threshold.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence in two files. Registry.sol's addVault initializes the dummy position with type(uint256).max. Registry.sol's internal updateHoldingPosition also uses type(uint256).max for new positions. TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime iterates over these positions looking for the minimum, but the minimum of all type(uint256).max values is type(uint256).max. The zero-check fallback is dead code when any position exists (including the always-present dummy position).

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The dummy position at index 0 is initialized with positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max. This position always exists for every vault and will be included in getLatestUpdateTime's minimum search.

All new holding positions are initialized with positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max. Same-chain positions never have their timestamp updated via updateHoldingPostionWithTime, so they permanently retain this sentinel value.

The minimum search over positions containing type(uint256).max yields type(uint256).max. The fallback check (latestUpdateTime == 0) never triggers because the first iteration sets latestUpdateTime to type(uint256).max (from the dummy position). This defeats any staleness check comparing the return value against block.timestamp.

calling_sequence: 1. Vault is created via addVault, pushing dummy position with positionTimestamp=type(uint256).max
2. Same-chain connectors add holding positions via updateHoldingPosition, each with positionTimestamp=type(uint256).max
3. getLatestUpdateTime is called (e.g., by AccountingManager for TVL freshness check)
4. Loop starts: i=0 (dummy position), latestUpdateTime=0 so condition is true, latestUpdateTime=type(uint256).max
5. Loop continues for all same-chain positions: their positionTimestamp is also type(uint256).max, which is NOT less than latestUpdateTime, so no update

6. If a cross-chain position exists but its LayerZero update failed/is stale, its positionTimestamp is from the last successful update, which IS less than type(uint256).max, so latestUpdateTime updates to that stale time
7. If no cross-chain positions exist, latestUpdateTime remains type(uint256).max
8. The zero-check fallback is dead code since latestUpdateTime is type(uint256).max != 0
9. Function returns type(uint256).max, which passes any freshness check
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Registry.addVault pushes dummy with type(uint256).max -> Registry.updateHoldingPosition (internal) pushes new positions with type(uint256).max -> TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime iterates and returns type(uint256).max because all positions have the sentinel value and the zero-check fallback is unreachable.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show TVLHelper is deployed as a library and linked to AccountingManager. The deployAccountingManager.ts script deploys AccountingManager with TVLHelper library. No special configuration of timestamps is performed.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: (1) A vault operates with both same-chain and cross-chain positions. (2) Same-chain positions have positionTimestamp = type(uint256).max (default). (3) Cross-chain TVL update via LayerZero fails or is delayed. (4) Any downstream freshness check using getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max, which always passes staleness validation. (5) The system operates with stale cross-chain TVL data, potentially leading to incorrect share prices. Even in the simpler case where a vault has only same-chain positions, getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max, which is semantically incorrect as a 'latest update time' and would mislead any consumer expecting a reasonable timestamp.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The defect allows stale cross-chain TVL data to bypass freshness validation. If an AccountingManager (not in scope but referenced in deployment scripts as a consumer of TVLHelper) uses getLatestUpdateTime to gate operations on TVL freshness, this check is rendered useless. Attackers could exploit windows where cross-chain TVL is stale to deposit at deflated share prices or withdraw at inflated share prices. The severity is HIGH because the invariant that getLatestUpdateTime(vaultId) <= block.timestamp is violated whenever the vault has any positions.
recommended_mitigations: Filter out type(uint256).max values in getLatestUpdateTime: treat them as 'not yet updated' and skip them in the minimum search
Initialize new holding positions with positionTimestamp = block.timestamp instead of type(uint256).max
Add an explicit check at the end of getLatestUpdateTime: if (latestUpdateTime == type(uint256).max) latestUpdateTime = block.timestamp;
Consider requiring explicit timestamp setting for all positions that participate in freshness checks
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Registry and TVLHelper. 2. Add a vault via addVault. 3. Add a same-chain token holding position via a connector calling updateHoldingPosition. 4. Call getLatestUpdateTime and verify it returns type(uint256).max. 5. Assert that type(uint256).max > block.timestamp (always true), demonstrating the staleness check bypass.
uncertainties: The AccountingManager contract (primary consumer of getLatestUpdateTime) is not in scope, so the exact freshness check logic cannot be verified
The actual impact depends on how AccountingManager uses getLatestUpdateTime - whether it gates deposits/withdrawals based on this value
additional_testing_needed: Review AccountingManager source to verify how getLatestUpdateTime return value is consumed
Test with mixed same-chain and cross-chain positions to verify behavior when only some positions have real timestamps
Verify if any off-chain monitoring systems also rely on getLatestUpdateTime
